## Short answer

I did **not** obtain an explicit all-reducible counterexample. What I can prove is a fairly sharp obstruction:

> Any all-reducible positive-definite weighted tree, if it exists, must contain a vertex \(u\) such that every component of \(T-u\) has inverse-root entry \(<1\), and every reducing parity witness for \(u\) has coefficient \(|s|\ge 5\) at \(u\).  
> Moreover, the witness must contain a genuinely **high-odd resonant branch**. For the first possible case \(s=5\), one branch must have a vector \(X\) with root coefficient \(2\), norm \(5\), and inverse-root entry
> \[
> \frac45\le \alpha<\frac56.
> \]
> Nonbad branches from the exact-one-bad proof cannot supply such a branch.

So the multiple-bad question reduces to ruling out, or constructing, these narrow high-odd resonance branches.

---

## 1. Parity-coset criterion

Let \(u\) be a vertex. Then \(u\) is reducible iff there exists
\[
y\in u+2L(T),\qquad y\ne \pm u,\qquad y^2\le u^2.
\]

Indeed, if \(u=a+b\) with \(a,b\ne0\) and \(a\cdot b\ge0\), set \(y=a-b=2a-u\in u+2L(T)\). Then
\[
y^2=(a-b)^2=(a+b)^2-4a\cdot b=u^2-4a\cdot b\le u^2.
\]
Conversely, if such \(y\) exists, then
\[
a=\frac{u+y}{2},\qquad b=\frac{u-y}{2}
\]
are nonzero lattice vectors, \(u=a+b\), and
\[
a\cdot b=\frac{u^2-y^2}{4}\ge0.
\]

Also, any weight-one vertex is automatically irreducible, since a norm-one vector cannot split into two nonzero positive-norm vectors with nonnegative pairing. Thus an all-reducible counterexample has all weights \(\ge2\).

---

## 2. Decomposition of a witness at a vertex

Fix a vertex \(u\), put \(m=w(u)=u^2\), and write a parity witness as
\[
y=s u+2X,\qquad s\ \text{odd}.
\]
Replacing \(y\) by \(-y\) if necessary, assume \(s>0\). Let the components of \(T-u\) be \(C\). For each component, let \(r_C\) be the root adjacent to \(u\), write \(X_C\in L(C)\), and define
\[
c_C=(X_C)_{r_C},\qquad Q_C=X_C^2.
\]
Then
\[
y^2=m s^2+4\sum_C Q_C-4s\sum_C c_C.
\]
Set
\[
K=\frac{s^2-1}{4}.
\]
The condition \(y^2\le m\) is exactly
\[
\sum_C (s c_C-Q_C)\ge mK. \tag{1}
\]

For each component define the inverse-root entry
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]
Positive definiteness of \(T\) gives the Schur complement inequality
\[
\Gamma_u:=\sum_C\alpha_C<m. \tag{2}
\]
Also Cauchy gives
\[
c_C^2\le \alpha_C Q_C. \tag{3}
\]
Consequently,
\[
s c_C-Q_C\le \frac{s^2}{4}\alpha_C,
\]
and summing over \(C\) gives the necessary real bound
\[
\Gamma_u\ge m\left(1-\frac1{s^2}\right). \tag{4}
\]
Thus high-odd witnesses can occur only at vertices whose Schur complement \(m-\Gamma_u\) is very small.

---

## 3. There is always an \(\alpha\)-small vertex

For an oriented edge \(u\to v\), let \(\alpha_{v|u}\) be the inverse-root entry of the component containing \(v\) after deleting the edge \(uv\), rooted at \(v\). If deleting \(uv\) gives two rooted components with inverse-root entries \(\alpha_{u|v}\) and \(\alpha_{v|u}\), then positive definiteness gives
\[
\alpha_{u|v}\alpha_{v|u}<1. \tag{5}
\]

Therefore, in any finite positive-definite tree, there is some vertex \(u\) such that every component of \(T-u\) has
\[
\alpha_C<1.
\]
Indeed, if every vertex had some adjacent component with \(\alpha\ge1\), orient each vertex toward one such component. A finite directed tree with outdegree at least one has a directed \(2\)-cycle, forcing both inverse-root entries across one edge to be \(\ge1\), contradicting (5).

Call such a vertex **\(\alpha\)-small**.

---

## 4. Low-odd witnesses force an \(\alpha\ge1\) direction

Let \(u\) be reducible.

### Unit witnesses: \(s=1\)

If \(s=1\), then \(K=0\), and reducibility requires
\[
\sum_C(c_C-Q_C)\ge0.
\]
If some nonzero component has \(c_C-Q_C\ge0\), then \(Q_C\le c_C\), so \(c_C>0\), and by Cauchy,
\[
c_C^2\le \alpha_C Q_C\le \alpha_C c_C.
\]
Thus \(\alpha_C\ge c_C\ge1\).

So any \(s=\pm1\) witness points toward a component with \(\alpha\ge1\).

### Cubic witnesses: \(s=3\)

Now \(K=2\). Put
\[
F_C=3c_C-Q_C.
\]
If \(\alpha_C<1\), then \(F_C\le1\). Indeed, if \(F_C\ge2=K\), then \(F_C=K\) forces \(\alpha_C\ge1\) by the integer maximum
\[
\max_{c\in\mathbb Z}(3c-c^2)=2.
\]
Moreover, if \(F_C=1\), then Cauchy gives \(\alpha_C\ge1/2\).

If all components had \(\alpha_C<1\), then every positive contribution is at most \(1\), and each such contribution costs at least \(1/2\) in \(\alpha\). Since a witness requires
\[
\sum_C F_C\ge2m,
\]
we would get
\[
\Gamma_u=\sum_C\alpha_C\ge m,
\]
contradicting \(\Gamma_u<m\).

Thus any \(s=\pm3\) witness also forces some component with \(\alpha\ge1\).

### Consequence

If every reducible vertex admitted a witness with coefficient \(|s|\le3\), then choosing an \(\alpha\ge1\) direction from every vertex would give the same directed-cycle contradiction as above. Hence:

> If an all-reducible counterexample exists, then at an \(\alpha\)-small vertex every reducing witness must have \(|s|\ge5\).

This strictly strengthens the unit-coefficient obstruction from Task t4.

---

## 5. Sharp high-odd obstruction

Let \(s=2k-1\ge5\), so
\[
K=k(k-1)=\frac{s^2-1}{4}.
\]
For a component \(C\), write
\[
F_C=s c_C-Q_C.
\]
If \(F_C=t>0\), then \(c_C>0\), \(Q_C=sc_C-t\), and Cauchy gives
\[
\alpha_C\ge \rho_s(t):=
\min_{\substack{c\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\\ sc>t}}
\frac{c^2}{sc-t}. \tag{6}
\]

Now suppose \(u\) is \(\alpha\)-small and has a reducing witness with coefficient \(s\). Since \(\Gamma_u<m\), if every component satisfied
\[
F_C\le K\alpha_C,
\]
then summing would give
\[
\sum_C F_C\le K\Gamma_u<Km,
\]
contradicting (1). Therefore some component must satisfy
\[
F_C>K\alpha_C. \tag{7}
\]

For such a component, \(t=F_C\) must be a **resonant integer**. Indeed, if \(\rho_s(t)\ge t/K\), then
\[
\alpha_C\ge \rho_s(t)\ge \frac{t}{K},
\]
contradicting \(t=F_C>K\alpha_C\). The inequality \(\rho_s(t)<t/K\) is equivalent to the existence of an integer \(c>0\) such that
\[
(k-1)c<t<kc. \tag{8}
\]
So a high-odd counterexample must contain a branch contribution lying strictly between two consecutive multiples \((k-1)c\) and \(kc\).

### First possible case: \(s=5\)

Here \(k=3\), \(K=6\). The only resonant integer \(t<K\) is
\[
t=5,
\]
with \(c=2\). Therefore an \(\alpha\)-small \(s=5\) witness must contain a component with
\[
F_C=5,\qquad c_C=2,\qquad Q_C=5,
\]
and
\[
\frac45\le \alpha_C<\frac56. \tag{9}
\]
The lower bound is Cauchy:
\[
\alpha_C\ge\frac{c_C^2}{Q_C}=\frac45,
\]
and the upper bound is exactly \(F_C>K\alpha_C\), i.e.
\[
5>6\alpha_C.
\]

This is a very narrow target. Nonbad rooted branches from the exact-one-bad proof satisfy the stronger inequality
\[
F_C\le K\alpha_C,
\]
so they cannot supply such a component. Thus any all-reducible counterexample must use a genuinely bad rooted component realizing this high-odd resonance.

---

## 6. Minimality of the witness does not by itself kill high-odd cases

Let
\[
P=\sum_C c_C.
\]
If \(y=s u+2X\) is locally shortest among vectors obtained by replacing \(s\) by \(s\pm2\), then comparing norms gives
\[
\frac{m(s-1)}2\le P\le \frac{m(s+1)}2. \tag{10}
\]
For \(s=5\), this is
\[
2m\le P\le3m.
\]
The resonant atom above has \(F=5\) and \(c=2\), so its ratio \(c/F=2/5\). A formal collection with total contribution \(6m\) would have \(P=(2/5)6m=12m/5\), which lies inside \([2m,3m]\). Thus minimality alone does **not** force \(s=\pm1\) or \(\pm3\); one really has to rule out or realize the resonant branches.

---

## Current conclusion

I cannot certify an all-reducible counterexample from the available examples. The strongest rigorous reduction I get is:

> Any all-reducible positive-definite weighted tree with at least one bad vertex must have an \(\alpha\)-small vertex whose reducing witnesses all have \(|s|\ge5\). For the first possible high-odd case \(s=5\), a branch must contain an integral vector with root coefficient \(2\), norm \(5\), and inverse-root entry in the narrow interval \([4/5,5/6)\).

So the remaining problem is precise: either prove that no rooted positive-definite weighted tree can realize these resonant windows, or construct enough such branches to build and verify a global counterexample.

The target lemma is **true as stated**. A slightly stronger dual parity statement gives exactly the missing odd “parity slack.”

## Theorem

Let \(C\) satisfy the hypotheses, let \(A\) be its Gram matrix, let \(G=A^{-1}\), let \(e_r\) be the root coordinate vector, and set

\[
y=G e_r,\qquad \alpha=G_{rr}=y_r.
\]

Then \(0<\alpha\le 1\), and for every \(x\in \mathbb Z^C\), with \(c=x_r\) and \(Q=x^T A x\),

\[
2n c-Q\le n^2\alpha
\]

for every \(n\in\mathbb Z\), with equality iff

\[
x=nA^{-1}e_r
\]

is integral. Also, for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\),

\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

In fact, equality in the odd inequality occurs iff

\[
x=kA^{-1}e_r
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=(k-1)A^{-1}e_r
\]

as an integral vector.

## Key dual parity lemma

The main point is the following stronger statement.

**Dual parity lemma.**  
If \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) satisfies

\[
\lambda_r\equiv 1\pmod 2,\qquad \lambda_u\equiv 0\pmod 2\quad(u\ne r),
\]

then

\[
\lambda^T G\lambda\ge \alpha.
\]

Moreover equality holds iff \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).

### Proof of the dual parity lemma

We prove simultaneously by induction on \(|C|\) that \(\alpha\le 1\) and the dual parity lemma holds.

If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(A=[w(r)]\), \(\alpha=1/w(r)\le 1\), and for odd \(\lambda_r=s\),

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda=\frac{s^2}{w(r)}\ge \frac1{w(r)}=\alpha,
\]

with equality iff \(s=\pm1\).

Now suppose \(r\) has child branches \(C_i\), with child roots \(r_i\), matrices \(A_i\), inverses \(G_i=A_i^{-1}\), and

\[
\alpha_i=(G_i)_{r_i r_i}.
\]

Each \(C_i\) again satisfies the same rooted hypotheses: the child root gains the extra \(+1\) because its degree drops by one when the edge to \(r\) is removed. By induction, \(\alpha_i\le 1\). The Schur complement gives

\[
\alpha=\frac1S,\qquad S=w(r)-\sum_i \alpha_i.
\]

If \(d\) is the number of children of \(r\), then

\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i\ge (d+1)-d=1,
\]

so \(\alpha\le 1\).

Now take \(\lambda\) with the parity pattern above. Write

\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_d),
\]

where \(s=\lambda_r\) is odd and \(\mu_i\in\mathbb Z^{C_i}\). Let \(e_i\) denote the root coordinate vector of \(C_i\), and define

\[
a_i=e_i^TG_i\mu_i,\qquad E_i=\mu_i^TG_i\mu_i,\qquad A_0=\sum_i a_i.
\]

A block inverse / Schur complement computation gives

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

For each branch, apply the induction hypothesis to the two parity vectors

\[
2\mu_i-e_i,\qquad 2\mu_i+e_i.
\]

Both have odd coordinate at the branch root and even coordinates elsewhere. Hence

\[
(2\mu_i-e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i-e_i)\ge \alpha_i
\]

and

\[
(2\mu_i+e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i+e_i)\ge \alpha_i.
\]

Expanding gives

\[
4E_i-4a_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i,\qquad
4E_i+4a_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i,
\]

so

\[
E_i\ge |a_i|.
\]

Therefore

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i |a_i|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}
\ge
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

It remains to use the elementary scalar inequality

\[
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}\ge \frac1S
\]

for \(S\ge1\) and odd \(s\). Multiplying by \(S\), this is

\[
4S|A_0|+(s+2A_0)^2\ge 1.
\]

Set \(y=2A_0\). Since \(s\) is odd, \(|s|\ge1\). If \(sy\ge0\), then \(|s+y|\ge |s|\ge1\). If \(sy<0\), write \(a=|s|\), \(u=|y|\). Then the left side is

\[
(a-u)^2+2Su.
\]

For \(a=1\), this is

\[
1+u^2+2(S-1)u\ge1.
\]

For \(a\ge3\), either \(u\le a-1\), in which case \((a-u)^2\ge1\), or \(u>a-1\), in which case \(2Su\ge2u>2(a-1)\ge4\). Thus the scalar inequality holds.

Equality forces \(s=\pm1\) and \(A_0=0\). Then the earlier inequalities force \(\sum_iE_i=0\), hence every \(\mu_i=0\). Therefore \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). This proves the dual parity lemma.

## Odd inequality

Let

\[
t=2k-1.
\]

For \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), define

\[
\lambda=2Ax-t e_r.
\]

Because \(A\) is integral, \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\). Moreover, \(\lambda_r\) is odd and every nonroot coordinate is even. Hence the dual parity lemma gives

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge \alpha.
\]

But

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
(2Ax-t e_r)^TG(2Ax-t e_r)
=
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha.
\]

Thus

\[
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha\ge \alpha,
\]

so

\[
tc-Q\le \frac{t^2-1}{4}\alpha.
\]

Since \(t=2k-1\),

\[
\frac{t^2-1}{4}=k(k-1),
\]

and therefore

\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

Equality holds iff \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). If \(\lambda=e_r\), then

\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=e_r,
\]

so

\[
Ax=k e_r,\qquad x=kA^{-1}e_r.
\]

If \(\lambda=-e_r\), then

\[
Ax=(k-1)e_r,\qquad x=(k-1)A^{-1}e_r.
\]

That proves the sharp odd equality statement.

## Even inequality

This one is the ordinary real quadratic bound. For \(n\in\mathbb Z\),

\[
0\le (x-ny)^TA(x-ny)
=
Q-2n c+n^2\alpha.
\]

Hence

\[
2n c-Q\le n^2\alpha.
\]

Equality holds iff \(x=ny=nA^{-1}e_r\), and since \(x\) is required to be integral, equality occurs exactly when that vector is integral.

## Equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\)

For \(k=1\), the odd equality alternatives are

\[
x=A^{-1}e_r
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=0.
\]

For \(k=0\), they are

\[
x=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=-A^{-1}e_r.
\]

Thus nonzero equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces \(y=A^{-1}e_r\) to be integral. Since \(y_r=\alpha\) and \(0<\alpha\le1\), integrality forces \(\alpha=1\).

If \(\alpha=1\), then in the Schur complement formula

\[
1=S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i\ge (d+1)-d=1,
\]

so equality holds throughout: \(w(r)=d+1\) and every child branch has \(\alpha_i=1\). Descending recursively, one eventually reaches a one-vertex rooted branch with \(\alpha=1/w(v)=1\), hence \(w(v)=1\). Therefore nonzero equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces a norm-one vertex somewhere in \(C\). In particular, if all weights in \(C\) are at least \(2\), then for \(k=0,1\) the only equality case is \(x=0\).

Suppose there is an integral vector X in L(C) whose coefficient at r is 2 and whose norm is X^2=5. Can alpha be <5/6? A promising route is to decompose C at r. For each child component with root coefficient c_i and norm Q_i, use the even inequality 4c_i-Q_i<=4 beta_i, where beta_i is the child inverse-root entry, and analyze the integrality gap between 4 beta_i and 4c_i-Q_i. Equality forces the child vector to be 2 times the dual root vector when integral; exploit the resulting half-integrality restrictions on beta_i if possible. The target range alpha<5/6 is equivalent to the root Schur complement S=1/alpha>6/5, while X^2=5 gives a total gap 5-4S<1/5, so any positive gap must be extremely small. writeup_tasks.statement: Let T be a finite weighted tree with positive-definite lattice form. Suppose there is a unique vertex b such that w(b)<d_T(b). Then T contains a vertex that is irreducible in L(T); indeed, if T has a weight-one vertex then such a vertex is irreducible, and otherwise the unique bad vertex b is irreducible.

Define weighted tree, the lattice L(T), the bilinear form, positive definiteness, degree, bad vertex, and irreducible element. Do not refer to any prior task, shared knowledge base, or conversation. The proof should include all needed lemmas. Required structure: first prove that any weight-one vertex is irreducible, since a norm-one vector cannot split into two nonzero positive-norm vectors with nonnegative pairing. Then assume all weights are at least 2 and let b be the unique vertex with w(b)<d_T(b). Delete b. For each component C of T-b rooted at the neighbor of b, set A_C for its Gram matrix and alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{rr}. Explain why C is a rooted nonbad branch: the root has w(r)>=d_C(r)+1 and every nonroot vertex has w(x)>=d_C(x). Prove the rooted branch inequality: for every integral x_C in L(C), with root coefficient c_C and norm Q_C, and every integer k, one has (2k-1)c_C-Q_C <= k(k-1)alpha_C. Include the derivation from the dual parity lemma: for a rooted nonbad branch, every dual vector whose root coordinate is odd and all other coordinates are even has dual norm at least alpha_C; apply this to lambda=2A_C x_C-(2k-1)e_r and expand. You may prove the dual parity lemma by induction using Schur complements, as in the attached solver outputs. Next prove the Schur complement inequality sum_C alpha_C < w(b) for the block decomposition of the Gram matrix at b. Finally, for an arbitrary vector x in L(T), write x=k b+sum_C x_C and compute exactly x·(b-x)=k(1-k)w(b)+sum_C((2k-1)c_C-Q_C). Apply the branch inequality and sum alpha_C<w(b). For k not equal to 0 or 1, this gives x·(b-x)<0. For k=0 or 1, it gives x·(b-x)<=0, and the equality case plus the no-weight-one assumption forces x=0 or x=b. Conclude that b has no nontrivial decomposition b=a+c with a·c>=0, hence b is irreducible. Make the notation unambiguous and avoid using a and b both for vertices and summands.
